LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT ANTHONY KARAVIDAS' MOTION FOR ATTORNEY'S FEES AND COSTS IS CONTINUED TO AUGUST 7, 2018 TO GIVE MR. KARAVIDAS AN OPPORTUNITY TO PROVIDE ITS FEE AGREEMENT AND BILLING RECORDS WHICH STATE THE AMOUNTS THAT WERE BILLED TO MR. KARAVIDAS, INCLUDING THE ACTUAL HOURLY RATES FOR ALL PERSONS WHO PERFORMED LEGAL SERVICES WHICH MR. KARAVIDAS IS SEEKING FEES ON THIS MOTION. NO LATER THAN JULY 24, 2018 MR. KARAVIDAS MUST FILE ONE OR MORE SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATIONS PROVIDING: 1) HIS FEE AGREEMENT; 2) BILLING RECORDS THAT WERE PROVIDED TO HIM BY HIS COUNSEL; AND 3) AN EXPLANATION, IF THERE IS ANY, WHY MR. KARAVIDAS WAS BILLED AT LOWER HOURLY RATES THAN THE RATES THAT MR. KARAVIDAS ARGUES ARE THE PREVAILING RATES IN THE COMMUNITY FOR SIMILAR WORK. NO LATER THAN JULY 31, 2018 PLAINTIFF MUSIC GROUP COMMERCIAL BM LTD. MAY FILE SUPPLEMENTAL OPPOSITION PAPERS RESPONDING TO THE SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATIONS FILED BY MR. KARAVIDAS. CASE LAW, INCLUDING CASES RELIED ON BY MR. KARAVIDAS, ESTABLISHES THAT THE COURT "MAY CONSIDER THE TERMS OF THE PARTIES' CONTRACT" EVEN THOUGH THOSE TERMS MAY NOT BE DISPOSITIVE ON A FEES MOTION. (NEMECEK & COLE V. HORN (2012) 208 CAL. APP. 4TH 641, 649 QUOTING VELLA V. HUDGINS (1984) 151 CAL. APP. 3D 515, 520; SEE ALSO SYSERS III, INC. V. RANKIN (2014) 226 CAL. APP. 4TH 691, 702 ("THE TRIAL COURT WAS NEITHER REQUIRED TO FOLLOW THE LAFFEY MATRIX NOR TO ADOPT THE RATE DEFENSE COUNSEL OPINED WAS THE 'MARKET RATE' FOR SERVICES OF THIS TYPE.")). JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: M.GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR #8600, TORREANOM@AOL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/HK)

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT DAVE SMITH INSTRUMENTS LLC'S MOTION FOR ATTORNEY'S FEES AND COSTS IS CONTINUED TO AUGUST 7, 2018 TO GIVE DSI AN OPPORTUNITY TO PROVIDE ITS FEE AGREEMENT AND BILLING RECORDS WHICH STATE THE AMOUNTS THAT WERE BILLED TO DSI, INCLUDING THE ACTUAL HOURLY RATES FOR ALL PERSONS WHO PERFORMED LEGAL SERVICES WHICH DSI IS SEEKING FEES ON THIS MOTION. NO LATER THAN JULY 24, 2018 DSI MUST FILE ONE OR MORE SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATIONS PROVIDING: 1) ITS FEE AGREEMENT; 2) BILLING RECORDS THAT WERE PROVIDED TO IT BY ITS COUNSEL; AND 3) AN EXPLANATION, IF THERE IS ANY, WHY DSI WAS BILLED AT LOWER HOURLY RATES THAN THE RATES THAT DSI ARGUES ARE THE PREVAILING RATES IN THE COMMUNITY FOR SIMILAR WORK. NO LATER THAN JULY 31, 2018 PLAINTIFF MUSIC GROUP COMMERCIAL BM LTD. MAY FILE SUPPLEMENTAL OPPOSITION PAPERS RESPONDING TO THE SUPPLEMENTAL DECLARATIONS FILED BY DSI. CASE LAW, INCLUDING CASES RELIED ON BY DSI, ESTABLISHES THAT THE COURT "MAY CONSIDER THE TERMS OF THE PARTIES' CONTRACT" EVEN THOUGH THOSE TERMS MAY NOT BE DISPOSITIVE ON A FEES MOTION. (NEMECEK & COLE V. HORN (2012) 208 CAL. APP. 4TH 641, 649 QUOTING VELLA V. HUDGINS (1984) 151 CAL. APP. 3D 515, 520; SEE ALSO SYSERS III, INC. V. RANKIN (2014) 226 CAL. APP. 4TH 691, 702 ("THE TRIAL COURT WAS NEITHER REQUIRED TO FOLLOW THE LAFFEY MATRIX NOR TO ADOPT THE RATE DEFENSE COUNSEL OPINED WAS THE 'MARKET RATE' FOR SERVICES OF THIS TYPE.")). Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs
Off Calendar Aug-22-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance by any party or counsel.

There being no appearances, the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Dave Smith Instruments LLC's motion for attorneys' fees and costs is granted in part. DSI is awarded fees and costs against plaintiff Music Group Commercial BM Ltd. in the amount of $40,845.92, calculated as 124 hours of Mr. Shryrock's time at the billed hourly rate of $185 plus 110.6 hours of Ms. Varnica's time at the billed hourly rate of $170 less a negative .1 multiplier for all the attorney hours plus costs of $3278.12. The billed hourly rates represent the market rate for the time spent by DSI's attorneys on DSI's successful anti-SLAPP motion. There is no reason why DSI, its insurer or its attorneys should reap an unearned windfall because of their success on the anti-SLAPP motion. If DSI, its insurer or its attorneys believe that the attorneys' hourly rates are below market, they could have negotiated for what they believe are market rates. Per the standard of what generally constitutes a market rate - what an attorney and client freely negotiate without external constraints - the billed hourly rates are the market rates. The hours claimed by DSI's attorneys are reduced by the time identified in Music Group's initial opposition memorandum for work spent on matters other than the anti-SLAPP motion. The resulting number of hours is reduced by a negative multiplier of .1 to express the court's considerable distaste for the extraordinarily excessive request made by DSI in lieu of the court exercising its discretion to award no fees at all due the excessive request. Music Group has not shown that any of the costs sought by DSI are not recoverable. Order signed. (see order for full details)

MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DAVE SMITH INSTRUMENTS LLC'S Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs.

Defendant Dave Smith Instruments LLC's motion for attorney's fees and costs is continued to August 7, 2018 to give DSI an opportunity to provide its fee agreement and billing records which state the amounts that were billed to DSI, including the actual hourly rates for all persons who performed legal services which DSI is seeking fees on this motion. No later than July 24, 2018 DSI must file one or more supplemental declarations providing: 1) its fee agreement; 2) billing records that were provided to it by its counsel; and 3) an explanation, if there is any, why DSI was billed at lower hourly rates than the rates that DSI argues are the prevailing rates in the community for similar work. No later than July 31, 2018 plaintiff Music Group Commercial BM Ltd. may file supplemental opposition papers responding to the supplemental declarations filed by DSI. Case law, including cases relied on by DSI, establishes that the court "may consider the terms of the parties' contract" even though those terms may not be dispositive on a fees motion. (Nemecek & Cole v. Horn (2012) 208 Cal. App. 4th 641, 649 quoting Vella v. Hudgins (1984) 151 Cal. App. 3d 515, 520; see also Sysers III, Inc. v. Rankin (2014) 226 Cal. App. 4th 691, 702 ("the trial court was neither required to follow the Laffey Matrix nor to adopt the rate defense counsel opined was the 'market rate' for services of this type.")).

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Music Group Commercial BM Ltd.'s Motion for Reconsideration of the October 19, 2017 orders granting the defendants' anti-SLAPP motions is continued to April 19, 2018 at 9:30 AM in Department 302. Music Group is allowed to propound discovery on the first prong issues relating to the application of the commercial speech exemption of whether a) defendant Anthony Karavidas was primarily engaged in the business of selling or leasing products at or near the time he made the allegedly libelous statements; b) defendant Dave Smith Instruments LLC authorized Mr. Karavidas to make the allegedly libelous statements; and c) Mr. Karavidas made the allegedly libelous statements for the purpose of advancing the commercial interests of DSI. It is contemplated that this discovery will be completed no later than March 16, 2018. Music Group may file supplemental moving papers no later than March 26, 2018, defendants may file supplemental opposition papers no later than April 4, 2018, and Music Group may file supplemental reply papers no later than April 11, 2018. The court has done a full review of the defendants' anti-SLAPP motions. The court confirms the portions of the October 19, 2017 orders that: a) defendants satisfied their first prong burden that all of Music Group's claims arise from protected activity encompassed within CCP 425.16(e)(3) and b) Music Group did not satisfy its second prong burden of showing that any of its claims have minimal merit. The court vacates the portions of those orders that Music Group failed to show that Mr. Karavidas made the allegedly libelous statement and has decided that this "alternative ground" for a second prong ruling should not have been addressed in the October 19, 2017 orders. This leaves the application of the commercial speech exemption as the only remaining issue. As to that issue, in the exercise of the court's discretion, Music Group's request to take discovery is granted on the grounds that Music Group has shown good cause for the taking of the discovery previously referred to in this order. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Notice Of Motion And Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiff Complaint
Off Calendar Oct-19-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Andrew Chang, Esq. (415-544-1900) of Shook Hardy & Bacon LLP for Plaintiff.
Forrest Shyroch, Esq. (415-398-0200) of Vogl Meredith Burke LLP for Defendant Dave Smith Instruments.
Stephen Ellingson, Esq. (650-637-9100) of Hayes Scott Bonino Ellingson McLay LLP for Defendant Anthony Karavidas.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court inquires of Plaintiff whether commercial speech applies. Plaintiff argues and cites case law on first prong and second prong. Defendant argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ANTHONY KARAVIDAS' Motion And Special Anti-Slapp Motion To Strike Plaintiff Complaint

Defendant Anthony Karavidas's anti-SLAPP motion to strike plaintiff Music Group Commercial BM Ltd.'s complaint is granted as to all claims alleged against Mr. Karavidas. Mr. Karavidas satisfied his first prong burden of showing that all of Music Group's claims against him arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute because all of the claims are based on statements that were made in a public forum on an issue of public interest. CCP 425.16 (e)(3). The allegedly libelous statements made by Mr. Karavidas were posted on an internet forum freely accessible to the general public, which constitutes a public forum. The statements were on matters of public interest because they concerned Music Group's business practices and the qualities and design of its products, issues that had been topics of numerous online postings, discussion threads, and press releases. Music Group has failed to show that the statements by Mr. Karavadis fall within the commercial speech exemption of CCP 425.17 (c). That exemption, which is narrowly construed, requires that defendant speaker himself engage in the business of selling products and does not apply to a speaker acting on behalf of or as an agent of a person that is in the business of selling products. (Simpson Strong-Tie Company, Inc. v. Gore (2010) 49 Cal.4th 12, 22; All One God Faith, Inc. v. Organic and Sustainable Industry Standards, Inc. (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 1186, 1213). Music Group has not shown that Mr. Karavadis is a person engaged in the business of selling or leasing products and has not alleged that DSI is the speaker. Music Group has not satisifed its second prong burden of showing that any of its claims against Mr. Karavidas have minimal merit. The specific language and the overall context of the statements made by Mr. Karavadis show that those statements are nonactionable opinions. Music Group has not shown that a reasonable fact finder could conclude any of the statements are provably false factual assertions. The language of the statements reveals their subjectivity: "lack of integrity", "underhanded business practices", "obvious history of copying", "looks like a blatant copy to me," "I can't have an opinion which differs from the fanboys? I'm not saying anything that isn't true. Look at the cable tester. It's a copy I can upvote anything I want." Mr. Karavadis' statement that he has been an audio engineer for years made in a forum for audio enthusiasts bolsters the credibility of his opinions, but does not make them factual assertions. The context of the forum is informal, with voluminous posts and reposts. Further, because Music Group is a limited public figure, it is required to show by a preponderance of evidence that Mr. Karavidas made the statements with actual malice, which it failed to do. Music Group also failed to allege pecuniary loss resulting from Mr. Karavidas' statements with the required specificity to state an actionable claim for trade libel.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Lewis Gillian , CSR No:12355 , email: lgillian@sftc.org. (D302)

MINI MINUTES: Appearances:

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court inquires of Plaintiff whether commercial speech applies. Plaintiff argues and cites case law on first prong and second prong. Defendant argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT DAVE SMITH INSTRUMENTS LLC's SPECIAL MOTION To Strike Slapp Suit.

Defendant Dave Smith Instrumental LLC's anti-SLAPP motion to strike plaintiff Music Group Commercial BM Ltd.'s complaint is granted as to all claims alleged against DSI. DSI satisfied its first prong burden of showing that all of Music Group's claims against it arise from activity protected by the anti-SLAPP statute because all of the claims are based on statements that were made in a public forum on an issue of public interest. CCP 425.16 (e)(3). The allegedly libelous statements made by Mr. Karavidas were posted on an internet forum freely accessible to the general public, which constitutes a public forum. The statements were on matters of public interest because they concerned Music Group's business practices and the qualities and design of its products, issues that had been topics of numerous online postings, discussion threads, and press releases. Music Group has failed to show that the statements by Mr. Karavadis fall within the commercial speech exemption of CCP 425.17 (c). That exemption, which is narrowly construed, requires that defendant speaker himself engage in the business of selling products and does not apply to a speaker acting on behalf of or as an agent of a person that is in the business of selling products. (Simpson Strong-Tie Company, Inc. v. Gore (2010) 49 Cal.4th 12, 22; All One God Faith, Inc. v. Organic and Sustainable Industry Standards, Inc. (2010) 183 Cal.App.4th 1186, 1213). Music Group has not shown that Mr. Karavadis is a person engaged in the business of selling or leasing products and has not alleged that DSI is the speaker. Music Group has not satisifed its second prong burden of showing that any of its claims against DSI have minimal merit. Music Group offered no response to DSI's argument that Mr. Karavadis did not make the allegedly defamatory statements within the scope of his employment to support liability against DSI based on a theory of vicarious liability. Moreover, the specific language and the overall context of the statements made by Mr. Karavadis show that those statements are nonactionable opinions. Further, because Music Group is a limited public figure, it is required to show by
a preponderance of evidence that Mr. Karavidas made the statements with actual malice, which it failed to do. Music Group also failed to allege pecuniary loss resulting from Mr. Karavidas' statements with the required specificity to state an actionable claim for trade libel. (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
